title: Population
highYieldFacts: Population is the total number of people in an area at a given time.
Population density = Total population ÷ Area (persons per sq km).
Crude Birth Rate (CBR) = live births per 1,000 people per year.
Crude Death Rate (CDR) = deaths per 1,000 people per year.
Natural increase ≈ Birth rate − Death rate (per 1,000).
Total population change = natural increase + net migration.
Asia is the most populous continent.
A population pyramid shows age and sex structure.
Expansive pyramid: wide base, young population, high birth rate.
Contractive pyramid: narrow base, ageing population, low birth rate.
Overpopulation strains resources—not merely high numbers.
Underpopulation means fewer people than resources can support efficiently.
Sparse population occurs in deserts, mountains, and polar regions.
Fertile river valleys attract dense settlement.
Industrial and urban areas raise local density.
Life expectancy has risen due to better health care.
Infant mortality reflects quality of medical care.
Family planning helps stabilise population growth.
Education for women tends to lower birth rates.
Net migration = immigrants − emigrants.
Arithmetic density is the standard measure at school level.
Bangladesh is among the world's most densely populated countries.
Australia is large but sparsely populated overall.
India's population concentrates in the northern plains.
High density can exist without overpopulation if resources are adequate.
Overpopulation may cause pollution and deforestation.
Census counts population periodically in India.
Demography is the statistical study of population.
Ageing populations need more elderly care.
Youthful populations need more schools and future jobs.

notes.content:
**Executive summary:** Human numbers have grown rapidly since industrialisation; most people live in Asia and Africa today.

**Must know**
• World population exceeded 8 billion in the 2020s
• Asia has the largest share of global population
• Growth rate is slowing in many countries but absolute numbers still rise
• Medical advances and better food supply reduced death rates
• Distribution is uneven—dense belts vs sparsely settled regions

**Population** is the total number of people living in a country or region at a given time.

For centuries growth was slow. After the **Industrial Revolution**, improved **health care**, **sanitation**, and **agriculture** lowered death rates while birth rates stayed high—causing rapid increase.

Today patterns differ: Japan and parts of Europe grow slowly; many African nations still grow quickly. **India** and **China** are among the world's most populous countries.
notes.explanation:
Population growth varies by region and time period—always state where and when.

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch2-02 — next topic
notes.teacherTip: Use a world population clock to show real-time growth.
notes.examTip: ICSE often asks which continent is most populous and why growth rates differ.

notes.content:
**Executive summary:** Population density compares how many people live per unit area, usually per square kilometre.

**Must know**
• Formula: Density = Total Population ÷ Area (sq km)
• High density: Bangladesh, Netherlands, Gangetic plain of India
• Low density: Sahara, Amazon interior, Siberia, interior Australia
• High density does not always mean overpopulation
• Arithmetic density is the standard school-level measure

**Population density** = Population ÷ Area.

**Example:** 50 million people in 100,000 sq km → 50,000,000 ÷ 100,000 = **500 persons/sq km**.

Factors raising density: fertile soil, water, flat land, jobs, good transport.
Factors lowering density: deserts, mountains, extreme cold or heat, lack of water.
notes.explanation:
Density is a ratio—show both population and area in calculations.

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch2-01 — previous topic; geo-rev-ch2-03 — next topic
notes.teacherTip: Compare density of Kerala, Rajasthan, and Bihar using atlas data.
notes.examTip: Show working: Population ÷ Area = persons per sq km.

notes.content:
**Executive summary:** Birth rate and death rate measure live births and deaths per 1,000 people per year.

**Must know**
• Crude Birth Rate (CBR): live births per 1,000 population per year
• Crude Death Rate (CDR): deaths per 1,000 population per year
• Rates are per 1,000, not percentages at this level
• Developing countries often have higher CBR than developed countries
• Better health care lowers CDR and raises life expectancy

**Crude Birth Rate** counts live births yearly per 1,000 people.
**Crude Death Rate** counts deaths yearly per 1,000 people.

Related measures: **infant mortality rate** (deaths under age one per 1,000 live births) and **life expectancy** (average years a person is expected to live).
notes.explanation:
Per-1,000 rates let countries of different sizes be compared fairly.

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch2-02 — previous topic; geo-rev-ch2-04 — next topic
notes.teacherTip: Classroom analogy: 1,000 students, 25 births, 8 deaths → net +17.
notes.examTip: Define CBR and CDR with the per-1,000 formula.

notes.content:
**Executive summary:** Natural increase equals birth rate minus death rate and drives population change before migration.

**Must know**
• Natural Increase ≈ Birth Rate − Death Rate (per 1,000)
• Positive natural increase means population grows without migration
• Negative natural increase means deaths exceed births
• Total change = natural increase + net migration
• Net migration = immigrants minus emigrants

**Natural increase** = CBR − CDR.

Example: CBR 22, CDR 7 → natural increase = **15 per 1,000** (about 1.5% per year).

**Total population change** = Natural increase + Net migration.
notes.explanation:
Separate natural increase (births/deaths) from total growth (includes migration).

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch2-03 — previous topic; geo-rev-ch2-05 — next topic
notes.examTip: State units: per 1,000 per year unless percentage is asked.

notes.content:
**Executive summary:** Age-sex pyramids show how a population is distributed across age groups and gender.

**Must know**
• Males usually on left, females on right, by age cohorts
• Broad base → high birth rate, young population (expansive)
• Narrow base → low birth rate, ageing population (contractive)
• Bulge in middle may reflect past baby boom or migration
• Used to plan schools, jobs, health care, and pensions

| Pyramid shape | Meaning |
|---------------|---------|
| Wide base, narrow top | Young, fast-growing population |
| Even columns | Stable growth |
| Narrow base, wide top | Ageing, slow or declining growth |

**Expansive** pyramids need more schools; **contractive** pyramids need elderly care.
notes.explanation:
Read pyramids from base (children) to top (elderly).

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch2-04 — previous topic; geo-rev-ch2-06 — next topic
notes.teacherTip: Sketch three pyramid types with country examples.
notes.examTip: Describe shape and link to birth rate and future workforce.

notes.content:
**Executive summary:** Overpopulation occurs when people exceed the carrying capacity of an area, straining resources.

**Must know**
• Depends on resources, not numbers alone
• Pressure on food, water, housing, jobs, and sanitation
• Environmental stress: pollution, deforestation, waste
• Urban slums and congestion in megacities
• Solutions include education, family planning, and sustainable development

**Overpopulation** = too many people relative to **available resources**.

**Effects:** unemployment, overcrowded schools/hospitals, pollution, loss of green cover, water stress.

**Solutions:** family planning, girls' education, better farming, planned cities, conservation.
notes.explanation:
Overpopulation is relative to resources—a wealthy dense city may cope better than a poor crowded region.

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch2-05 — previous topic; geo-rev-ch2-07 — next topic
notes.teacherTip: Contrast high density with adequate vs inadequate resources.
notes.examTip: List economic, social, and environmental effects separately.

notes.content:
**Executive summary:** Underpopulation means too few people to use resources efficiently; sparse areas have very low density.

**Must know**
• Underpopulation: labour shortage, underused farmland or minerals
• Sparse population: few people over vast area (deserts, tundra)
• Causes: harsh climate, poor soil, remoteness, lack of water
• Australia and Canada are large but relatively underpopulated
• Governments may encourage settlement in resource-rich sparse areas

**Sparse population** = low density due to difficult physical conditions.

**Underpopulation** = population smaller than the area's **resource potential**.

Examples: **Sahara**, **Amazon interior**, **Ladakh**, **Siberia**.
notes.explanation:
Sparse = geography limits people; underpopulated = could support more productively.

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch2-06 — previous topic; geo-rev-ch2-08 — next topic
notes.teacherTip: Map world's sparse vs dense population belts.
notes.examTip: Give physical reasons for sparse settlement.

notes.content:
**Executive summary:** People are unevenly spread; physical and human factors explain clustering and empty lands.

**Must know**
• Dense on fertile plains, river valleys, and coasts
• Sparse in mountains, deserts, and polar regions
• Industry and trade pull people to urban centres
• Transport, history, and government policy also matter
• India's density is highest in the northern plains

**Physical factors:** climate, relief, soil, water, vegetation.

**Human factors:** farming, industry, trade, policy, migration.

**India:** dense along Ganga-Brahmaputra and coastal plains; lower in arid Rajasthan and high Himalayas.
notes.explanation:
Distribution shows where people live; density shows how many per sq km.

**🔗 Links:** geo-rev-ch2-07 — previous topic
notes.teacherTip: Overlay rainfall and population maps of India.
notes.examTip: Name at least two physical and two economic factors.

questions.question: Population density is calculated as:
questions.answer: Density = total population divided by land area, expressed as persons per sq km.

questions.question: Crude Birth Rate measures:
questions.answer: CBR counts live births per 1,000 population each year.

questions.question: Natural increase equals:
questions.answer: Natural increase = CBR − CDR before migration is counted.

questions.question: Which continent has the largest population?
questions.answer: Asia contains more than half of the world's people (China, India, etc.).

questions.question: A wide-based population pyramid indicates:
options: High birth rate and young population
questions.answer: Broad base means many children—expansive pyramid.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:
1. Overpopulation depends only on high population density.
2. Overpopulation means people exceed available resources.
Which of the above is/are correct?
questions.answer: Statement 1 is false; statement 2 is correct—overpopulation is resource-relative.

questions.question: Sparse population is commonly found in:
questions.answer: Harsh climate and lack of water limit settlement in deserts and tundra.

questions.question: Crude Death Rate is expressed per:
questions.answer: Vital rates use per 1,000 population per year.

questions.question: Net migration equals:
questions.answer: Positive net migration means more people arriving than leaving.

questions.question: An ageing population typically shows a pyramid with:
questions.answer: Few young people and many elderly—contractive pyramid.

questions.question: Which factor most attracts dense rural settlement?
questions.answer: Agriculture needs fertile land and reliable water.

questions.question: Demography is the study of:
questions.answer: Demographers analyse size, structure, and change of population.

questions.question: Life expectancy measures:
options: Average years a person is expected to live
questions.answer: Higher life expectancy reflects better health care and living conditions.

questions.question: India conducts a national population Census every:
questions.answer: Census of India is held every ten years.

questions.question: Underpopulation may lead to:
questions.answer: Too few workers can slow economic use of resources.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:
1. CBR is live births per 1,000 per year.
2. CDR is deaths per 1,000 per year.
3. Density is births minus deaths.
Which of the above is/are correct?
questions.answer: Statements 1 and 2 are correct; density is population ÷ area.

questions.question: Bangladesh is known for:
questions.answer: Small area with large population → very high density.

questions.question: Family planning aims to:
options: Help couples plan number and spacing of children
questions.answer: Voluntary planning stabilises growth and improves health.

questions.question: Infant mortality rate indicates:
options: Deaths of infants under one year per 1,000 live births
questions.answer: Lower infant mortality shows better maternal and child health care.

questions.question: A population with CBR 30 and CDR 8 has natural increase of:
questions.answer: 30 − 8 = 22 per 1,000 per year.

questions.question: Which region of India is generally most densely populated?
questions.answer: Fertile alluvial soil and water support dense farming settlements.

questions.question: Expansive population pyramid is typical of:
questions.answer: High birth rates produce a broad young base.

questions.question: Total population change includes:
questions.answer: Growth = (B−D) + net migration.

questions.question: Arithmetic population density uses:
questions.answer: Standard density = total population ÷ total area.

questions.question: Education of girls often leads to:
questions.answer: Education delays marriage and improves family planning.

questions.question: Siberia has sparse population mainly due to:
options: Extreme cold and difficult living conditions
questions.answer: Harsh climate limits comfortable settlement.

questions.question: Overcrowded slums are often linked to:
options: Rapid urban growth without adequate housing
questions.answer: Rural-urban migration can outpace infrastructure.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:
1. Australia has a large land area.
2. Australia is densely populated throughout.
Which of the above is/are correct?
questions.answer: Statement 1 is true; statement 2 is false—much of Australia is arid and sparsely settled.

questions.question: Contractive pyramid suggests need for:
questions.answer: Few young people and many old—ageing society.

questions.question: Which is NOT a push factor for migration?
questions.answer: Better jobs elsewhere is a pull factor, not a push.

questions.question: World population growth was fastest after:
questions.answer: Falling death rates with initially high birth rates spurred growth.

questions.question: High population density in Netherlands is partly due to:
options: Long habitable history, trade, and fertile reclaimed land
questions.answer: Fertile lowlands and economic development support dense settlement.

questions.question: Negative natural increase means:
questions.answer: Deaths exceed births—population shrinks without immigration.

questions.question: Population distribution refers to:
options: How people are spread across an area
questions.answer: Distribution describes spatial pattern of where people live.

questions.question: Carrying capacity relates to:
options: Maximum population an environment can sustain
questions.answer: Overpopulation exceeds carrying capacity of resources.

questions.question: Census data helps government to:
questions.answer: Accurate counts guide resource allocation.

questions.question: Youth bulge in a pyramid means:
options: Many people in young age groups
questions.answer: Large young cohort needs jobs and education.

questions.question: Which pair has the correct formula?
questions.answer: Only natural increase formula is correctly stated.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:
1. Fertile plains attract dense settlement.
2. Deserts usually have sparse population.
3. Mountains always have highest density.
Which of the above is/are correct?
questions.answer: Statements 1 and 2 correct; mountains generally have lower density except some valleys.

questions.question: India's population growth rate in recent decades has:
questions.answer: Family planning and development slowed growth though numbers still rise.

questions.question: Primary reason for high density in river valleys:
options: Fertile alluvial soil and water for farming
questions.answer: Agriculture supported early civilisations along rivers.

questions.question: Overpopulation can cause:
questions.answer: Resource stress harms environment and living standards.

questions.question: A country with CBR 12 and CDR 10 has:
Population pyramid horizontal bars show:
options: Number of males and females in each age group
questions.answer: Each bar represents an age cohort by sex.

questions.question: Sparse population in Amazon interior is due to:
options: Dense forest, difficult access, and disease history
questions.answer: Tropical rainforest limits large-scale settlement.

questions.question: Demographic transition generally involves:
options: Shift from high birth/death rates to lower rates
questions.answer: Development lowers both rates over time.

questions.question: Which is a social effect of overpopulation?
questions.answer: Services strain when population exceeds provision.

questions.question: Underpopulated countries may encourage:
questions.answer: More workers needed to develop resources.

questions.question: Population of a city compared to its area gives:
questions.answer: Same density formula applied to urban area.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:
1. CBR and CDR are measured per 1,000 people per year.
2. Population density is measured per 1,000 people.
Which of the above is/are correct?
questions.answer: Statement 1 true; density is per sq km, not per 1,000.

questions.question: Age-sex pyramid with equal narrow columns suggests:
questions.answer: Similar cohort sizes indicate stability.

questions.question: Gangetic plain density is high mainly because of:
questions.answer: Agricultural heartland of India.

questions.question: Excess population pressure on farmland may cause:
options: Soil erosion and smaller farm plots
questions.answer: Subdivision and overuse degrade land.

questions.question: Which vital rate reflects medical advancement most directly?
questions.answer: Better hospitals and vaccines reduce infant deaths.

questions.question: Population census provides data on:
questions.answer: Census is comprehensive demographic survey.

questions.question: High death rate in a developing country may be due to:
questions.answer: Poor health infrastructure raises CDR.

questions.question: Population pyramid is also called:
questions.answer: Shows age and sex structure graphically.

questions.question: Overpopulation in cities may worsen:
options: Both congestion/pollution and reduce open space
questions.answer: Urban crowding affects transport, air quality, and living space.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:
1. Net migration affects total population.
2. Natural increase ignores migration.
3. Density equals CBR minus CDR.
Which of the above is/are correct?
questions.answer: Statements 1 and 2 correct; density is population ÷ area.

questions.question: A region with 2 million people and 4,000 sq km has density:
Pull factor for rural to urban migration includes:
options: Lack of schools in village only as push
questions.answer: Better employment draws people to towns.

questions.question: Stationary population pyramid has:
questions.answer: Low growth produces near-rectangular shape.

questions.question: World's population is unevenly distributed mainly because of:
questions.answer: People cluster where life and livelihood are easier.

questions.question: Family welfare programmes in India promote:
questions.answer: Voluntary planning improves mother and child health.

questions.question: Low population density in Rajasthan desert is due to:
questions.answer: Thar Desert conditions limit agriculture and settlement.

questions.question: Population explosion refers to:
options: Rapid increase in population over short period
questions.answer: Post-1950 rapid growth in many developing nations.

questions.question: Which indicator suggests a youthful nation?
questions.answer: Many young people lower median age.

questions.question: Overpopulation differs from high density because it considers:
options: Resource availability and quality of life
questions.answer: Resource pressure defines overpopulation.

questions.question: Canada's overall low density is explained by:
options: Large area with much inhospitable northern land
questions.answer: Most people live near US border; north is sparse.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:
1. Population pyramids help plan future school needs.
2. Population pyramids show contour heights.
Which of the above is/are correct?
questions.answer: Statement 1 true; pyramids show age-sex structure, not relief.

questions.question: Population density is calculated as Population ÷ Area.
questions.answer: True. Density expresses how many people live per unit area, usually per sq km.

questions.question: Crude Birth Rate measures deaths per 1,000 people per year.
questions.answer: False. CBR measures live births per 1,000 per year; CDR measures deaths.

questions.question: A wide-based population pyramid indicates a young, fast-growing population.
questions.answer: True. Many children at the base mean high birth rates—expansive pyramid.

questions.question: Overpopulation depends only on the number of people, not on resources.
questions.answer: False. Overpopulation is defined relative to available resources and services.

questions.question: Natural increase equals Birth rate minus Death rate.
questions.answer: True. It shows growth from births and deaths before migration.

questions.question: Deserts typically have sparse population.
questions.answer: True. Lack of water and extreme climate limit settlement.

questions.question: Asia is the most populous continent.
questions.answer: True. China, India, and other Asian nations hold the majority of world population.

questions.question: Underpopulation can mean too few workers to use resources efficiently.
questions.answer: True. Labour shortage may leave farmland or minerals underused.

questions.question: Net migration equals Immigrants minus Emigrants.
questions.answer: True. Positive net migration adds to population; negative subtracts.

questions.question: A contractive pyramid has a very broad base and narrow top.
questions.answer: False. Contractive pyramids have a narrow base (few children) and wider top (many elderly).

questions.question: Population density = Total population ÷ ______.
questions.answer: Area (in sq km). Result is persons per sq km.

questions.question: Crude Birth Rate is the number of live births per ______ people per year.
questions.answer: 1,000 (per thousand population).

questions.question: Natural increase ≈ Birth rate − ______ rate.
questions.answer: Death rate (CBR − CDR per 1,000).

questions.question: A graph showing age and sex structure is called a population ______.
questions.answer: Population pyramid (age-sex pyramid).

questions.question: The statistical study of population is called ______.
questions.answer: Demography.

questions.question: Match terms with definitions:
questions.answer: CBR→births; CDR→deaths; Density→per area; Natural increase→difference.

questions.question: Match pyramid types:
questions.answer: Expansive→wide base; Contractive→narrow base; Stationary→even; Youth bulge→young adults.

questions.question: Match regions with density pattern:
questions.answer: Ganga→high; Sahara→sparse; Bangladesh→very high; Siberia→sparse.

questions.question: Match concepts:
questions.answer: Over→resource strain; Under→labour shortage; Census→count; Demography→study.

questions.question: Match indicators:
questions.answer: Life expectancy→lifespan; Infant mortality→under-1 deaths; Net migration→in minus out.

questions.question: Match factors with dense settlement:
questions.answer: Soil→farming; Water→supply; Flat→construction; Port→employment.

questions.question: Match sparse areas with cause:
questions.answer: Thar→dry; Himalayas→relief; Amazon→forest; Antarctica→cold.

questions.question: Match formulas:
questions.answer: Standard demographic equations.

questions.question: Match effects of overpopulation:
questions.answer: Multi-dimensional effects.

questions.question: Match continents:
questions.answer: Continental population patterns.

questions.question: Define population density and give the formula.
questions.answer: Population density is the number of people per unit area. Formula: Density = Total Population ÷ Area (sq km). Result is expressed as persons per sq km.

questions.question: Distinguish between overpopulation and high population density.
questions.answer: High density means many people per sq km. Overpopulation means people exceed available resources and services, lowering quality of life. A densely populated area with strong economy may not be overpopulated.

questions.question: Explain what an expansive population pyramid indicates.
questions.answer: An expansive pyramid has a broad base showing many children and young people, indicating high birth rate and rapid population growth. It implies future need for schools, jobs, and health services.

questions.question: Give three factors that lead to sparse population.
questions.answer: Extreme climate (desert cold or heat), difficult relief (high mountains), and lack of water or fertile soil limit settlement. Remoteness and poor transport also discourage dense population.

questions.question: How is natural increase calculated and what does it show?
questions.answer: Natural increase = Crude Birth Rate − Crude Death Rate (per 1,000 per year). It shows population change from births and deaths alone, excluding migration. Positive value means population grows naturally.
